Science.title: Environmental plan is largely off track, warns watchdog
Science.summary: A report says many of the measures are "well within the reach" of government and it was a matter of "moving away from planning into delivery".

Science.title: Survey finds 'significantly more' ancient woodland
Science.summary: A wildlife trust says there are 50% more sites in Hertfordshire than previously recorded.

Science.title: Every time Norway scores, the whole city of Bergen shakes
Science.summary: During Norway's first match of the FIFA World Cup against Iraq on June 17, several small vibrations were recorded on the seismometer. A particularly clear signal was observed when Erling Haaland scored.

Science.title: Brown seaweed flour enhances nutritional value and digestibility of gluten-free cookies
Science.summary: Flour made from the brown seaweed Sargassum filipendula can increase the nutritional value and digestibility of gluten-free cookies. This conclusion is based on a study published in the journal Food Research International.

Science.title: Hundreds of schools plan closures ahead of red heat alerts
Science.summary: The temperature topped 34.6 C in Wisley, England, on Tuesday while Scotland and Northern Ireland saw their hottest days of the year.

Science.title: Elephants move closer to humans when droughts are sustained
Science.summary: If drought in an area persists longer, elephants move closer to areas near human settlements. This is the finding of research by biologist Irene Bouwman of Radboud University. During short-term droughts, the animals remain close to rivers and lakes and move less than normal.

Science.title: New biofilm mechanism in Bacillus cereus could reveal vulnerabilities in food poisoning bacterium
Science.summary: Scientists from the Department of Microbiology at the University of Malaga, who are also members of the Institute of Subtropical and Mediterranean Horticulture "La Mayora" (IHSM), have discovered a previously unknown mechanism that allows the bacterium Bacillus cereus, which i...

Science.title: Cheap, effective and dangerous: How Australian farmers came to depend on the toxic weedkiller paraquat
Science.summary: It is illegal to use paraquat in at least 74 jurisdictions worldwide, including the European Union, China, Malaysia, Brazil and, most recently, the U.S. state of Vermont.

Science.title: A new 'prescription' to address the environmental crisis
Science.summary: A new "prescription for the planet" aims to address the deepening environmental crisis.

Science.title: Simple acknowledgment boosts repeat customer participation in take-back programs
Science.summary: Companies may only need to send an acknowledgment message to boost repeat customer participation in recycling and reuse programs for used goods like laptops and coffee pods, according to a new study led by Penn State researchers. The idea for the study arose over a cup of coff...

Science.title: Listening for quantum oscillations in the Kondo insulator ytterbium dodecaboride
Science.summary: Magnetic quantum oscillations have been unexpectedly observed in insulators, where freely moving charge carriers are not expected to exist. A joint study by researchers from Tokyo University of Science, The University of Tokyo and Kobe University investigated this puzzling beh...

Science.title: Heat pump growth stalls as government support cut, warns climate watchdog
Science.summary: The growth in sales has slowed significantly after a critical government grant programme was cut.

Science.title: Four-decade mystery solved as PKCβ structure reveals new drug target
Science.summary: After nearly four decades of research, Mayo Clinic scientists have revealed the molecular structure of protein kinase C beta (PKCβ), a key protein linked to cancer and neurological diseases. The findings, published in Nature Communications, provide the first detailed view of h...

Science.title: Physical pressure helps pathogenic P. aeruginosa survive antibiotic treatment
Science.summary: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is an opportunistic pathogen that can cause acute and chronic infections. Responsible for many hospital-acquired infections, it is also a major concern for people with cystic fibrosis, whose lungs are clogged with thick mucus that promotes its growth.

Science.title: Researchers aim for hardier herds of worms for productive, resilient farming practice
Science.summary: The black soldier fly converts organic waste into biomass. The cricket brings crunchy protein to the dinner plate. The mealworm can break down plastic.

Science.title: People care more about being right than avoiding mistakes, study finds
Science.summary: Conventional wisdom says the best predictions are the ones that minimize mistakes, but new research suggests that is not necessarily how people see it. A study published in Management Science has found that when people make or evaluate predictions, they care more about the pos...

Science.title: Chloroplast study reveals molecular lock that helps power life on Earth
Science.summary: A new study reveals the dynamics of photosynthesis at the cellular level. Led by co-authors Professor Barry Bruce and Associate Professor Rajan Lamichhane, both of the Department of Biochemistry and Cellular and Molecular Biology (BCMB) at the University of Tennessee, Knoxvill...

Science.title: Brain growth may explain why birds lay outsized eggs compared with dinosaurs
Science.summary: A new study has uncovered a fundamental link between brain size and offspring size, helping to solve a long-standing evolutionary puzzle: Why do birds lay such disproportionately large eggs?

Science.title: Animals are often moved to make way for infrastructure, but we don't know what happens to them next
Science.summary: When major infrastructure projects are built in rural areas, wildlife is often displaced and moved out of the way. This is called mitigation translocation, and it is a globally recognized method for moving animals. However, not everyone applies it the same way, or at all.

Science.title: Self-driving chemistry lab discovers catalysts that can switch products on demand
Science.summary: Researchers have developed a self-driving chemistry lab that can autonomously search through hundreds of catalyst recipes and reaction conditions to identify faster, more selective and more programmable ways to make important industrial chemicals. The work could accelerate cat...

Science.title: Heat wave sparks health warnings across Europe
Science.summary: Workers sweated in choking heat and pupils stayed home Tuesday as an early-summer heat wave smothered much of Europe, with France suffering its hottest day on record.

Science.title: Pterosaur wing tests suggest modern reconstructions miss major shape diversity
Science.summary: Pterosaurs, the first vertebrates to fly, would have had more diverse wing shapes than current scientific reconstructions suggest, according to new University of Bristol-led research. The study is published in the journal Paleobiology.

Science.title: A Loophole Brings Cystic Fibrosis Patients a ‘Miracle Drug’ in Generic Form
Science.summary: A generic version of a breakthrough cystic fibrosis drug, manufactured in Bangladesh for a fraction of the American price, may give some families around the world an unlikely lifeline.

Science.title: New study highlights benefits of crop diversification in arable farming
Science.summary: Which farming strategies can help agricultural operations better manage yield risks caused by climate change and fluctuating prices in the agricultural product market? A new study by ZALF published in the journal Agricultural Systems uses model calculations to show that farms ...

Science.title: Graphene plasmon cavities enable advanced and scalable terahertz photodetectors
Science.summary: How could we noninvasively distinguish between healthy and cancerous tissue? And how could we increase the speed of wireless communications? These two seemingly unrelated questions may share the same answer: terahertz (THz) light. Spanning frequencies between 0.3 and 20 THz, T...

Science.title: Wave-packet interferometry captures elusive dark excitons in organic superconductor

Science.title: New algorithm identifies disease-linked changes in cells without prior training
Science.summary: A new algorithm could drive breakthroughs in understanding cancer, Alzheimer's disease and other potentially fatal conditions. Researchers from the University of Waterloo developed the machine-learning algorithm, called RNovA, to detect changes in the proteins in human cells. ...

Science.title: A partner's touch can feel unsafe for people with a history of childhood maltreatment
Science.summary: In romantic relationships, touch is usually considered part of everyday life: a quick back rub while cooking dinner, a hug after a long day at work or a lingering kiss before falling asleep. It's a simple way to show support, express affection and communicate sexual desire.

Science.title: Oldest known asteroid impact on Earth dated to 3 billion years
Science.summary: Curtin University researchers have determined the most precise age yet for the oldest known impact crater on Earth, providing new insight into how meteorite strikes shaped the planet during its earliest history.

Science.title: Unique instruments automate sample preparation, quality control for cryo-electron microscopy

Science.title: Researchers provide economic evidence base for shift away from animal testing
Science.summary: Researchers at Northumbria University have produced new economic evidence demonstrating the significant and growing commercial value of alternatives to animal testing in the U.K., with findings already featuring in parliamentary debate on the issue.

Science.title: Discovery of how cells maintain their DNA could shield key healthy cells from chemotherapy side effects

Science.title: Hubble details early galaxy transforming neighborhood 1.4 billion years after Big Bang

Science.title: Summer's silent killer: Why the world's heat waves are a global health emergency

Science.title: Controlling ice crystal growth using polymer nanoparticles
Science.summary: Ice formation can damage biological samples, tissues and materials during freezing and thawing. In nature, specialized molecules known as ice-binding proteins prevent ice crystals from growing too large, helping organisms survive in extreme cold. Scientists have long tried to ...

Science.title: Crop diversity and perennial grains could strengthen soil health under climate stress, study finds
Science.summary: A McGill University study suggests that diversifying crops and replacing annual wheat with a perennial grain could help protect soil health as climate change brings more variable rainfall. The research is published in the journal Applied Soil Ecology.

Science.title: Moose are native to Colorado, study shows
Science.summary: The modern Colorado moose is often considered just that: modern—brought to the state by wildlife officials in the late 1970s, preceded by very occasional reports of moose sightings in the 19th and early 20th centuries.

Science.title: Branched silver sensor offers more sensitive light-based drug measurements in blood plasma

Science.title: Surprising diversity found among Europe's last Neanderthals
Science.summary: A new study published in Nature provides the most detailed picture to date of Neanderthal diversity in Western Europe shortly before their extinction.

Science.title: Webb pinpoints millions of stars within Cigar galaxy
Science.summary: Located 12 million light-years away and undergoing rapid star formation, edge-on spiral galaxy Messier 82 (M82) is a scientifically unique sight to behold, and now NASA's James Webb Space Telescope has revealed previously unseen details.

Science.title: Plant protein pair reveals new wood-formation mechanism
Science.summary: Researchers from the Biosciences Department at Durham University have discovered a previously unknown mechanism that helps plants control the formation of wood, a finding that could open new directions for research into plant growth, productivity and carbon storage. The study,...

Science.title: DNA loops reveal how immune cells build millions of antibodies from one genome
Science.summary: How does your body produce millions of antibodies from one genome? New research reveals how two closely related proteins help immune cells fold DNA, connecting distant genetic pieces to create diverse antibodies that help fend off disease.

Science.title: Light-based sensors detect extremely low levels of traumatic brain injury biomarkers
Science.summary: Researchers have developed a chip-based metasurface biosensor that can detect traumatic brain injury (TBI) biomarkers at extremely low levels. With further development, the technology could one day help doctors make a faster diagnosis after a head injury, helping to guide trea...

Science.title: Antibiotic resistance threatens vision in pets and horses, veterinary review warns
Science.summary: Sight-threatening antibiotic-resistant eye infections are becoming a significant threat to vision in dogs, cats and horses, according to a new comprehensive review published in Veterinary Ophthalmology by researchers from the Hebrew University of Jerusalem. The study examines ...

Science.title: Horseshoe bats use echolocation to separate background echoes from those of fluttering prey
Science.summary: Many bat species emit echolocation calls and use the returning echoes to find their way, detect the presence of fluttering insects, and locate and catch them. A new study investigated this behavior in greater horseshoe bats foraging in the wild. An international team, includin...

Science.title: The fuel crisis has hit the Pacific hard. The region is responding—but tough choices lie ahead
Science.summary: The past five years have not been easy for the people of the Pacific. COVID restrictions disrupted tourism and upended supply chains, while global fuel shocks raised prices and hit island economies hard.

Science.title: Next-generation pesticide disrupts bumblebee reproduction
Science.summary: Bumblebees are only an inch long, but they help power the global food system. Roughly one-third of the food we grow depends on pollinators like bees—and those bees are regularly decimated by pesticides.

Science.title: New AI tool identifies wild animals by their unique patterns in real time
Science.summary: Patterns reveal the individual: A novel AI algorithm equips researchers with a powerful new tool to accurately identify individual jaguars, zebras and giraffes in real time based on their unique coat patterns. Biologists and ecologists can now observe individual wild animals a...

Science.title: Hundreds of schools close as UK braces for record-breaking heat wave
Science.summary: The UK braced for a record-breaking heat wave Tuesday as hundreds of schools closed early for the next two days and train companies slashed services.

Science.title: Researchers find microplastics in hedgehogs—then trace them back to pet food
Science.summary: When colleagues and I found microplastics in hedgehog droppings, we wanted to know where they were coming from. One answer was surprisingly close to home: pet food.

Science.title: Europe: the world's fastest-warming continent
Science.summary: The latest heat wave sweeping across Europe is a stark reminder that it is the world's fastest-warming continent, stretching into an Arctic that is heating at an even greater pace.

Science.title: India monsoon sweeps north but brings less rain than usual
Science.summary: Long-awaited monsoon rains arrived in India's financial capital, Mumbai, on Tuesday, cooling weeks of blazing heat despite persistent fears of water shortages, with total rainfall so far staying below the long-term average.

Science.title: Happy Asteroid Day! Prize-winning plan focuses on space infrastructure
Science.summary: For decades, astronomers and policymakers have been working on plans to protect our planet from killer asteroids. But now there's a new realm to protect: the thousands of satellites we're putting in orbit.

Science.title: Sawdust, cellulose binders and beeswax combine into eco-friendly foam
Science.summary: Polystyrene—common in packing peanuts and box inserts—is manufactured from fossil fuels. To develop a sustainable alternative, researchers reporting in ACS Applied Polymer Materials tested an unconventional starting material: sawdust. Their prototype foams incorporated cellulo...

Science.title: Yellow mealworms mapped anatomically for the first time
Science.summary: The dried larvae of the yellow mealworm beetle (Tenebrio molitor) are comparable to beef or poultry in nutritional value, but the mealworm has a far smaller ecological footprint. It was recently approved for human consumption by the European Food Safety Authority.

Science.title: Pathway to high-fidelity quantum computing identified
Science.summary: Researchers from the University of Sydney, working with IBM, have identified and quantified important factors limiting the performance of quantum computers and demonstrated ways to overcome their impact.

Science.title: Attitudes, not personality, may drive deepfake pornography creation
Science.summary: New research from Edith Cowan University (ECU) suggests attitudes, particularly those that excuse harmful behavior, may be a stronger predictor of willingness to create deepfake pornography than personality traits. The findings are published in the Journal of Sexual Aggression.

Science.title: Although woodland salamanders have looked the same for millions of years, their physiology has evolved rapidly
Science.summary: For her doctoral dissertation, Yale's Nathalie Alomar decided to study a small amphibian that appeared to have eluded the forces of evolution. She found that there is more to its evolution than meets the eye.

Science.title: Workplace structure impacts gender pay gap, study finds
Science.summary: New Adelaide University research has identified which industries, workplace structures and employment policies are most strongly linked to gender pay gaps in Australian workplaces, with flexible work arrangements being a key indicator.

Science.title: Stop asking whether Pride is a protest or a party, say researchers

Science.title: New method helps online ads reach overlooked groups
Science.summary: Online advertisers and government agencies use algorithmic tools to tailor and target their campaigns to reach as many people as possible.

Science.title: Energy Dept. Promises $17.5 Billion in Loans for Nuclear Power
Science.summary: The federal government wants to help utilities buy large components for up to 10 large nuclear reactors. It’s unclear which, if any, companies might participate.

Science.summary: Researchers have identified "DNA switches" that become active as honeybee larvae grow into worker bees, offering new insight into the development of these important pollinators and the ecosystems they support.

Science.title: From cool-down spots to chalk on windows - how Europeans are coping with the heat

Science.title: Red squirrel sickness reports under investigation
Science.summary: Tests are being carried out to establish if there is an outbreak of squirrelpox in the Borders.
